content:
The functional form **Σ p log(p/q)** appeared in **Kullback** and **Leibler's 1951** paper on information and sufficiency, building on **Shannon's 1948** entropy framework. The quantity became known as **Kullback–Leibler divergence** or **relative entropy**.

**Solomonoff**, **Rissanen**, and others later connected minimum description length and model selection to coding length, where KL measures redundancy of using the wrong code.

In statistics, **expected log-likelihood ratios** (the same object as KL) underpin **Neyman–Pearson** optimality and **Bahadur** efficiency. **Csiszár** developed a general theory of **information divergences** including KL as the canonical member.

Machine learning adopted KL through **cross-entropy loss**, **EM** and **variational inference** ( **Jordan et al.** ), **VAEs** ( **Kingma & Welling** ), and modern **RLHF** policy constraints — making D_KL(P || Q) a standard training primitive beyond classical information theory.

timeline.label: Shannon entropy (1948)
timeline.description: Entropy H(P) formalizes average uncertainty; KL later quantifies excess cost when Q replaces P.

timeline.label: Kullback & Leibler (1951)
timeline.description: Introduce the directed information measure now written D_KL(P || Q).

timeline.label: Variational inference in ML (1990s–2000s)
timeline.description: KL terms appear in ELBO optimization, linking Bayesian inference to tractable neural training objectives.

timeline.label: Deep generative models & RLHF (2010s–2020s)
timeline.description: VAE KL penalties and policy KL constraints become everyday tools in generative AI pipelines.
